content.text: Chat UI for GenAI Applications
A chat interface is the most common user-facing component of a GenAI product. It differs from a standard form-and-list UI in one critical way:
responses arrive incrementally
via server-sent events. The UI must manage streaming state, auto-scroll behaviour, markdown rendering, and optimistic updates — all without blocking the main thread.

attrs.alt: Chat UI component architecture: ConversationView, InputBar, state management, SSE stream, and auto-scroll rules

content.text: Component Structure
A minimal but complete chat UI is composed of three logical layers:
ConversationView:
a scrollable list of message bubbles, each rendered from a Message object with role (user/assistant), content, and status.
InputBar:
a growing textarea that submits on Enter (Shift+Enter inserts a newline), disables during streaming, and shows a Stop button when a stream is active.
State:
holds messages[], isStreaming, pendingText (the in-flight assistant chunk), and error.
React Implementation
Message Types and State
Streaming with fetch + ReadableStream
Modern browsers support streaming via
fetch + ReadableStream
. This approach works with both SSE and NDJSON without needing
EventSource
(which doesn't support custom headers like Authorization):
ConversationView with Auto-Scroll
Auto-scroll to the bottom during streaming but pause when the user scrolls up:
InputBar with Auto-Resize and Keyboard Handling
Markdown Rendering for Assistant Messages
Assistant responses typically contain markdown (headers, code blocks, lists). Use
react-markdown
with
remark-gfm
(GitHub Flavored Markdown) and
react-syntax-highlighter
for code:
Accessibility and UX Considerations
Mark the conversation list with role='log' aria-live='polite' so screen readers announce new messages.
Announce streaming progress with an aria-live='assertive' region that updates with each chunk.
The Send button must have a visible label (not just an icon) and be keyboard-focusable.
Disable the textarea during streaming to prevent accidental double-sends; indicate this visually.
Preserve conversation history in localStorage or IndexedDB so the user doesn't lose context on page refresh.
Show an error banner with a Retry button rather than silently failing when the SSE stream errors.

The most important streaming UX decision is the auto-scroll policy. Scroll to the bottom while the user is at the bottom (following the response); stop scrolling the moment they scroll up (they are reading something earlier). Failing to honour this makes the interface feel like it is fighting the user.
